title: ABI, Shared Libraries, and Foreign-Function Memory · Memory Repair Bay
summary: An incident responder inherits a corrupted versioned cross-language ownership ABI, the earliest failing input, and two plausible hypotheses; they must preserve first-failure evidence, repair the cause, and add a regression. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in versioned cross-language ownership ABI and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Characterize calling convention, symbol visibility, integer widths, alignment, layout, and compiler flags for each supported ABI.
Expose stable opaque handles with versioned constructors, operations, observers, and same-library destruction.
Pass buffers with lengths, ownership direction, allocator family, and release callback across FFI.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

Produce bounded C evidence to validate a versioned foreign buffer and allocator family; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Treat WebAssembly pointers as offsets into bounded linear memory with copy, growth, lifetime, and host-reference rules.

For Publish the incident record · Debug Wasm Linear Memory, challenge the documented misconception with changed-size, failure-injection, printed invariant, compiler, sanitizer, analyzer, fuzz, ABI, concurrency, or production-transfer evidence: A WebAssembly pointer is a host pointer and remains valid after memory growth.
